nativeRange.habitat: Found in fast-flowing rivers and streams with submerged driftwood and rocky substrates, specifically in the Apuré and Caroní River basins within the broader Orinoco drainage of Venezuela.

diet.notes: The Clown Pleco is xylivorous, meaning it actually consumes wood as part of its diet — driftwood provides cellulose that the fish requires for digestion and is not simply a decoration. Supplement the diet with algae wafers, blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, and peas, and occasional protein-rich treats like bloodworm or daphnia. The Clown Pleco also tends to graze on biofilm and algae that grow naturally on tank surfaces. Because this species is largely nocturnal, feeding is best done after lights out.

avoidWith: multiple male plecos in small tanks without sufficient caves

imageCandidates.notes: Jarne Colman; 1024x768. Correctly-ID'd live Clown Pleco resting among driftwood, sharp head with mottled markings and eye clearly visible. Caveat: warm-lit head close-up, so the full body and diagnostic wavy pale banding are only partly shown; kept as the only usable CC image available for this species.

summary: The Clown Pleco is a small, beginner-friendly catfish from Venezuela's Orinoco drainage, reaching around 7–10 cm. It relies on driftwood as part of its diet and must have access to it at all times — an excellent alternative to the Common Pleco.
careNotes: Driftwood is essential for the Clown Pleco's wellbeing — it provides cellulose for digestion and surfaces to graze biofilm from, so multiple pieces should be available at all times. This species is largely nocturnal, tending to hide in caves or under wood during the day and becoming active after lights out. Males tend to be territorial over cave sites, particularly during breeding; providing at least one cave per fish generally reduces conflict. The Clown Pleco is sold in the trade under several L-numbers: L104 and L162 are the most commonly encountered, with L262 also in circulation. L448, L206, and the larger 'Mega Clown' (L340) are separate but closely related variants that are sometimes confused with the Clown Pleco at point of sale. Like most catfish, the Clown Pleco is sensitive to copper-based medications, which should be avoided. At 7–10 cm, it makes a practical small-tank alternative to the Common Pleco (Hypostomus plecostomus), a species that can exceed 50 cm in length.
breedingNotes: The Clown Pleco is a cave spawner: the male selects and guards a small cave or tube, tends the eggs, and continues to watch over the newly hatched fry until they are free-swimming. Mimicking a rainy-season trigger — a slight temperature drop paired with a water change using softer water — has been reported to encourage spawning. The species has been bred in captivity with moderate success, and a species-only or low-competition setup tends to improve results.

primary.name: Fishkeeping World
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (no dedicated Clown Pleco species page found). Provides comprehensive species-level husbandry data.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Confirmed adult size 8.75–10 cm, temperature 23–28°C, pH 6.8–7.6, lifespan 10–12 years, cave-spawning breeding behavior, and nocturnal habits.

additional.name: Tankarium
additional.notes: Additional cross-check. Confirmed native range (Apuré and Caroní basins, Venezuela), L-number variants (L104, L162, L448), driftwood essentiality, and peaceful temperament.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority. Confirms scientific name Panaqolus maccus (Schaefer & Stewart, 1993), family Loricariidae, order Siluriformes, max size 8.8 cm SL, native to Apuré and Caroní River basins in Venezuela. IUCN Least Concern.

fish.breedingNotes: The male selects and guards eggs inside a cave or tube. Mimicking rainy-season conditions — a temperature drop paired with a soft-water change — has been reported to trigger spawning. The species has been bred in captivity with some regularity when kept in appropriate setups.
